Trading is basic math, and this video explains the four trading math concepts that decide whether a strategy becomes profitable or fails: expectancy, system design, variance, and risk. It shows why one trade means nothing, why win rate alone is misleading, and why long-term results come from system math rather than individual outcomes. It also covers the effect of transaction costs, sample size, breakeven math, position sizing, risk of ruin, and loss asymmetry on real account performance. The final section turns the lesson inward with a simple question about what you are doing wrong and what would change if you fixed it.

What’s covered in this video:

Why traders chase perfect entries while long-term success is decided by probabilities, math, and expectancy.
How expectancy is calculated from win rate, average win, and average loss, and why an 8R system with a low win rate can still be profitable.
Why expectancy on paper is not the same as real expectancy after spread, commission, and slippage.
Why small samples are misleading, why the first 10 to 30 trades can hide the truth, and why the larger sample reveals whether a system has edge.
How system design involves tradeoffs between reward and win rate, and why there is no perfect strategy that gives you both.
How the breakeven win-rate formula works, and why a trader can be wrong most of the time and still not lose money if the reward is large enough.
Why variance creates different equity curves for the same system, and why good execution can still lose in the short term.
Why gambler’s fallacy makes traders increase risk after a losing streak, even though each trade remains independent.
How position sizing keeps dollar risk consistent even when stop loss size changes, and why it matters more than most traders realize.
Why risk of ruin rises quickly as risk per trade increases, and why a 50% drawdown becomes mathematically much harder to recover from.

Mentioned in this video: trading math, profitability, expectancy, average profit per trade, win rate, average win, average loss, positive expectancy, system design, tradeoff, reward-to-risk, breakeven win rate, large sample, spread, commission, slippage, variance, equity curves, gambler’s fallacy, position sizing, stop loss size, risk per trade, risk of ruin, drawdown, loss asymmetry, probabilities, edge, coin flips, execution, prediction.
